title: Troubleshoot User Authorization Issue
description: Step-by-step process to diagnose and resolve a user access or authorization problem.

steps.action: Capture the failed authorization check
steps.reason: SU53 shows exactly which authorization object, field, and value was missing at the time of the last failed check. Run it immediately after the error — it only stores the most recent failure.

steps.action: Review the user's assigned roles and profiles
steps.reason: Confirm which roles are currently assigned to the user and that they are valid for the current date. A role with expired validity grants no access.

steps.action: Open the relevant role and check the authorization data
steps.reason: On the Authorization tab in PFCG, locate the missing authorization object from SU53. Check whether the required field value is maintained and not restricted.

steps.action: Add or correct the authorization and generate the profile
steps.reason: After editing the authorization, you must regenerate the authorization profile — the change has no effect until the profile is rebuilt and saved.

steps.action: Re-assign or refresh the role on the user if needed
steps.reason: Profile regeneration is usually sufficient. If the role was just added, verify the assignment saved correctly and validity dates are set.

steps.action: Ask the user to re-test the failing action
steps.reason: Confirm the fix worked. If SU53 shows a different failure, repeat the process — multiple missing authorizations may need to be resolved in sequence.

title: Create and Set Up a New SAP User
description: Complete process for creating a new SAP user, assigning roles, and verifying access.

steps.action: Create the user record with logon data and personal info
steps.reason: Set username, first/last name, email, and initial password. Choose the correct user type — Dialog for interactive users, Service for technical/batch users, Communication for RFC users.

steps.action: Assign the required roles on the Roles tab
steps.reason: Roles carry the authorization profiles. Assign all roles the user needs with correct validity dates. Always use roles rather than direct profile assignments.

steps.action: Confirm all assigned roles have current authorization profiles
steps.reason: A yellow or red status indicator on a role means the profile is outdated or missing. Regenerate the profile so the authorizations are active.

steps.action: Ask the user to log on and test their key transactions
steps.reason: Have the user attempt the main tasks they need to perform. If they hit authorization errors, use SU53 immediately after each failure to capture the missing object.

steps.action: Review any access failures and adjust roles via PFCG
steps.reason: Use the SU53 output to fine-tune authorizations in PFCG. Repeat until all required access is confirmed working.

title: Investigate Missing Transport Change
description: How to investigate why a transport imported successfully but the expected change is not visible in the target system.

steps.action: Check the transport import log in STMS
steps.reason: Navigate to the target system's import queue and open the import log. Look carefully for warnings — a green status icon does not mean the import was error-free.

steps.action: Verify the correct client and system were targeted
steps.reason: Customizing transports are client-specific. If the transport was imported to the wrong client, the change will not appear in the expected client.

steps.action: Open the transport request and check its object list
steps.reason: Expand the transport and verify the expected object (table entry, program, config) is actually in the request. If it is missing, it was never added to the transport.

steps.action: Check whether another transport overwrote the object
steps.reason: If multiple transports contain the same object, the last one imported wins. Check the import sequence for the object to detect overwrite conflicts.

steps.action: Re-import the transport if necessary
steps.reason: If the import had errors or targeted the wrong client, re-import from the STMS import queue with the correct settings. Check the log again after re-import.

title: Fix an OData Service Not Found Error
description: Diagnose and resolve a 404 or service unavailable error on an SAP Gateway OData service.

steps.action: Search for the service and check its activation status
steps.reason: The most common cause is the service not being activated on the Gateway. Search by service name and confirm it shows as active. If missing, add it from the backend.

steps.action: Verify the ICF service node is active
steps.reason: Even if activated in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, the underlying HTTP service node in SICF must also be active. Navigate to the OData service path and check its status.

steps.action: Clear the backend metadata cache
steps.reason: Stale backend cache causes the Gateway to fail when retrieving service metadata. Run this on the backend system first, before the frontend cache.

steps.action: Clear the Gateway frontend metadata cache
steps.reason: After clearing the backend cache, clear the Gateway hub cache too. Both must be cleared for a complete refresh of the service metadata.

steps.action: Verify the service definition is complete and regenerated
steps.reason: If the service was recently modified, confirm runtime objects were regenerated in SEGW. Without regeneration, model changes are not active in the Gateway.

steps.action: Test the $metadata URL in a browser or Gateway Client
steps.reason: Access /sap/opu/odata/svc/SERVICE_NAME/$metadata to confirm a valid XML response. Use the Gateway Client in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE for a quick test without leaving SAP.

title: Build and Activate a New OData Service
description: End-to-end flow for developing, registering, and testing a new OData service in SAP Gateway.

steps.action: Create or open the OData project and define the data model
steps.reason: Define entity types, properties, and associations that model your data. The data model determines the shape of the OData $metadata and all entity sets.

steps.action: Implement the DPC_EXT class methods for each entity set
steps.reason: The generated Data Provider Class extension (DPC_EXT) must be enhanced with ABAP code for each operation: GET_ENTITY, GET_ENTITYSET, CREATE_ENTITY, etc.

steps.action: Generate runtime objects
steps.reason: Use Generate → Runtime Objects after any model change. This creates the backend registration artifacts. Without this step, the service definition has no runtime effect.

steps.action: Register the service on the Gateway hub
steps.reason: Add the service in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE to expose it on the Gateway URL. Set the system alias to point to the backend where the service was built.

steps.action: Activate the ICF node if not already active
steps.reason: Ensure the HTTP service path for the OData service is active. Without an active ICF node, the Gateway returns HTTP 403 or 404 regardless of service registration.

steps.action: Use the SAP Gateway Client to test $metadata and entity sets
steps.reason: The built-in Gateway Client lets you call service endpoints without leaving SAP. Test $metadata first, then each entity set to confirm correct data is returned.

title: Investigate a Missing or Incorrect FI Document
description: How to find, review, and troubleshoot a financial accounting document that is missing, incorrect, or failed to post.

steps.action: Display the document by number, company code, and fiscal year
steps.reason: If you have the document number, start here to see exactly what was posted — header data, line items, G/L accounts, and posting keys.

steps.action: Search for the document via G/L account line items if number is unknown
steps.reason: FBL3N lets you filter by account, date, amount, and reference. Use it when you need to find a document without knowing its number.

steps.action: Check the G/L account balance to confirm whether the posting landed
steps.reason: If a posting should have hit a specific G/L account, verify the balance in FS10N. Period totals help confirm whether the document was included in the period.

steps.action: Check if the posting period is open for the document date
steps.reason: A closed posting period is one of the most common causes of posting failures. Ask an FI admin to check period settings in OB52 if the period appears closed.

steps.action: Check the system log if the document appears to have failed silently
steps.reason: Update task failures can cause documents to appear posted in the UI but not actually written to the database. SM21 will show update failures for the relevant timeframe.

title: Diagnose a Failed or Empty Payment Run
description: Step-by-step approach for investigating why the F110 automatic payment run did not select expected vendor invoices or produced no payments.

steps.action: Open the payment run and review the parameters
steps.reason: Check the company code, payment methods, vendor range, and next payment date. A single wrong parameter can exclude all expected vendors.

steps.action: Review the payment proposal log for exclusion reasons
steps.reason: The payment proposal displays each item considered and the reason it was excluded (payment block, no payment method, past due date, etc.).

steps.action: Check the vendor's open items directly
steps.reason: Confirm the invoice is open (not already cleared) and has no payment block indicator. FBL1N shows all open items with their attributes at a glance.

steps.action: Display the invoice and check the payment method and terms
steps.reason: The payment method on the invoice must match the payment run configuration. Also check that the due date falls within the 'Next payment date' window in F110.

steps.action: Remove any payment blocks and re-run the proposal
steps.reason: Once you have identified the exclusion reason, correct it (remove block, fix payment method, open period) and re-run the payment proposal to confirm selection.

title: Investigate a Blocked Sales Order
description: How to find and resolve delivery or billing blocks on a sales order that is stuck in processing.

steps.action: Display the sales order and check header-level blocks
steps.reason: On the Sales tab of the order header, look for Delivery Block and Billing Block fields. Note the block reason — this tells you the category of the problem.

steps.action: Check the Status Overview tab for the full document flow
steps.reason: The status overview shows whether a delivery or invoice was already created, and where the process is stuck. This prevents duplicate actions.

steps.action: If credit-blocked, contact the credit manager for release
steps.reason: Credit blocks are set automatically when the customer exceeds their credit limit. Only a credit manager should release these — do not remove them without authorization.

steps.action: Remove the delivery or billing block if authorized
steps.reason: In VA02, navigate to the header Sales tab and clear the block field. Save the order. The delivery or billing run can now pick up the order.

steps.action: Verify the block is removed and the order status updated
steps.reason: Re-display VA03 to confirm the block is gone and the order status allows further processing. Trigger delivery creation if needed.

title: Process and Verify a Goods Receipt
description: End-to-end steps for posting a goods receipt against a purchase order and verifying stock is updated correctly.

steps.action: Display the purchase order and confirm the expected delivery
steps.reason: Before posting the GR, verify the PO is open, quantities are correct, and no full GR has already been posted against the relevant line items.

steps.action: Post the goods receipt (movement type 101) referencing the PO number
steps.reason: In MIGO, select 'Goods Receipt' and 'Purchase Order'. Enter the PO number to load the expected items. Confirm quantities and storage location before posting.

steps.action: Note the material document number after posting
steps.reason: MIGO generates a material document number on successful posting. Record this for reference — it is needed for reversals or further investigation.

steps.action: Verify stock levels updated correctly
steps.reason: After posting, open MMBE for the material and plant to confirm unrestricted stock increased by the received quantity.

steps.action: Check the PO delivery status
steps.reason: Re-display the PO and check the Delivery Status on the item — it should now show as fully or partially delivered depending on the quantity received.

title: Diagnose an RFC Connection Failure
description: Step-by-step approach to diagnosing and resolving a failing RFC destination between SAP systems.

steps.action: Open the RFC destination and run the Connection Test
steps.reason: SM59 shows all configured RFC destinations. The 'Test Connection' button confirms whether the network layer is reachable. A failure here points to a network or hostname issue.

steps.action: Run Remote Login to test application-layer connectivity
steps.reason: If the connection test passes but Remote Login fails, the issue is with the logon credentials or the target system's availability — not the network.

steps.action: Verify the connection parameters: host, system number, client, and credentials
steps.reason: Even a small typo in the hostname or system number will prevent the connection. Confirm these values against the target system's instance profile.

steps.action: Check the system log on both sides for RFC errors
steps.reason: SM21 on the calling and target systems often reveals the root cause — authentication failures, authorization issues, or system-level connectivity errors.

steps.action: Confirm the target system RFC user has the correct authorizations
steps.reason: RFC technical users need authorization object S_RFC. Check SU53 on the target system if the connection test produces an authorization error.

steps.action: Save updated settings and re-test
steps.reason: After correcting parameters or permissions, save the RFC destination and run both tests again to confirm end-to-end connectivity is restored.

title: Investigate a Failed IDoc
description: Step-by-step approach for analyzing and reprocessing an inbound or outbound IDoc in error.

steps.action: Find the IDoc by status, message type, partner, or date
steps.reason: WE02 is the best starting point when you know an interface failed but do not yet know the exact root cause.

steps.action: Open the IDoc and review the current status record and error text
steps.reason: The status records usually show whether the problem is posting logic, master data, partner profile setup, or an application error.

steps.action: Refine the search if needed by message type or partner profile
steps.reason: WE05 is useful when you need broader filtering across many IDocs or want to confirm whether the issue affects multiple messages.

steps.action: Fix the underlying cause before retrying
steps.reason: Do not reprocess until the real issue is resolved, otherwise the IDoc will simply fail again with the same status.

steps.action: Reprocess the failed IDoc
steps.reason: BD87 is the standard transaction for retrying failed IDocs after the root cause has been corrected.

steps.action: Confirm the IDoc reached a successful status
steps.reason: Always verify that the final status is successful and that any downstream business document was actually created.

title: Investigate a Purchase Order Not Released
description: How to diagnose and resolve a purchase order that is waiting for approval or not progressing through release strategy.

steps.action: Display the purchase order and review the current release status
steps.reason: Start by checking whether the PO is actually subject to release strategy and which release code or status is pending.

steps.action: Check whether the document was changed after an earlier release
steps.reason: Certain changes reset the release status and force the approval process to start again.

steps.action: Confirm the responsible approver or release code is correct
steps.reason: If the strategy determination is wrong, the issue is often with document values, organizational data, or release configuration.

steps.action: Release the purchase order if you have the correct authorization
steps.reason: ME29N is the standard transaction for approving a PO in a release strategy process.

steps.action: Re-display the PO and confirm the release status updated
steps.reason: Always confirm the PO is now released and available for follow-on processing.

title: Investigate a Blocked Vendor Invoice
description: Step-by-step process for analyzing and releasing a blocked invoice after quantity or price variances are resolved.

steps.action: Locate the vendor invoice and confirm its current status
steps.reason: Start by confirming the invoice is open and identify whether it is blocked for payment or blocked due to invoice verification variances.

steps.action: Review the invoice details and compare against the PO and goods receipt
steps.reason: Most blocked invoices come from quantity or price mismatches between the invoice, PO, and GR.

steps.action: Correct the underlying variance if needed
steps.reason: Do not release the invoice until the business reason for the block has been resolved.

steps.action: Release the blocked invoice
steps.reason: MRBR is the standard transaction for releasing invoices blocked by invoice verification rules.

steps.action: Confirm the invoice is now eligible for payment
steps.reason: Recheck the vendor line item to ensure the block is gone and the invoice can be picked up by payment processing.

title: Investigate a Missing Workflow Item in Inbox
description: How to diagnose why an approval or workitem is not appearing in the user's SAP inbox.

steps.action: Check whether the workitem is already present in the user's inbox
steps.reason: Sometimes the issue is simply that the user is checking the wrong folder or has not refreshed the inbox.

steps.action: Search for the workflow workitem by task, user, or status
steps.reason: SWI5 helps determine whether the workitem exists and whether it is assigned, completed, or stuck.

steps.action: Verify whether an agent was determined for the workitem
steps.reason: If no agent is assigned, the workflow cannot appear in anyone's inbox.

steps.action: Use administrative processing if the workflow must be unblocked
steps.reason: SWIA allows an administrator to forward, execute, or complete a stuck workitem when normal processing is not possible.

steps.action: Ask the user to refresh the inbox and confirm the item is visible
steps.reason: Once the assignment or workflow issue is fixed, the user should verify that the item is now available for action.

title: Investigate Data That Did Not Save
description: How to diagnose a transaction that looked successful but did not actually commit data to the database.

steps.action: Search for failed update requests around the time of the issue
steps.reason: SM13 is the primary place to confirm whether the update task failed after the user saved the transaction.

steps.action: Open the failed update and review the error details
steps.reason: The update record often points directly to the program, function module, or business object that failed.

steps.action: Check for a related short dump
steps.reason: Many failed update requests also generate a dump, which gives more detailed technical information.

steps.action: Review the system log around the same timestamp
steps.reason: SM21 may show update terminations, enqueue issues, or system-level errors not obvious from the business transaction.

steps.action: Fix the root cause and repeat the business transaction if appropriate
steps.reason: Once the technical or data issue is resolved, the original business action may need to be entered again.
